title: The Art That Created Colonialism
gist: Reviewing Yale's 'Painters, Ports, and Profits' exhibition, researcher Carey K. Mott traces how the British East India Company — a merchant venture that grew a military twice the size of the British Army — used commissioned art as literal commercial intelligence and patronage, producing genuine cross-cultural artistic exchange in India while systematically omitting the Company's most violent economic activities (indigo coercion, opium, and the Bengal famine) from the visual record.

themes.theme: The Company as a corporate-military-intelligence hybrid

insights.claim: The British East India Company, founded in 1600 as an ordinary merchant venture, grew over two centuries into a corporation that also functioned as a military and administrative state, with a charter permitting war, diplomacy, and territorial annexation in service of its commercial interests; by the early 1800s its forces were twice the size of the British Army.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A historical extreme case of a private commercial entity accumulating state-scale military power under a corporate charter — a reference point for thinking about the limits of corporate power concentration.
insights.locator: The British East India Company was an ordinary merchant venture

insights.claim: Before the camera existed, the East India Company used commissioned artists as a form of corporate intelligence-gathering, dispatching them to document which commodities could be grown where, which trade routes were viable, and how to govern unfamiliar local populations.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: Illustrates an early, literal example of visual data collection functioning as market and governance intelligence — a historical precedent for how commercial actors have long used information-gathering to underwrite expansion.
insights.locator: But to properly exploit these markets

themes.theme: Art as evidence of genuine, if unequal, cross-cultural exchange

insights.claim: Indian artists such as Mihr Chand quickly adopted British techniques to make their work more marketable to European patrons — in the 1780s he copied an English oil painting by Tilly Kettle in traditional opaque watercolor — because Mughal painting's own centuries-old fusion of Persian, Central Asian, and Indigenous styles gave local artists an unusual capacity to 'metabolize rather than mimic' foreign conventions.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A concrete example of how a pre-existing hybrid artistic tradition let local producers capture value from a new colonial market rather than simply being displaced by it.
insights.locator: local artists had a particular capacity to absorb foreign conventions

insights.claim: Collaboration inside the Company's art networks was hierarchical and unequal in pay: Gangaram Tambat, hired to assist British artist James Wales in Pune, earned only a quarter of what Wales was paid despite blending traditional Mughal technique with skills learned from Wales.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A quantified pay-gap data point illustrating that cross-cultural artistic collaboration under the Company still ran on colonial economic hierarchy, not equal partnership.
insights.locator: For assistance, Wales hired Gangaram Tambat

insights.claim: The terms of trade shaped the terms of cultural exchange: India's more open, penetrative Company presence produced genuinely collaborative artist networks that fed into the Bengal Renaissance and an enduring Indo-European visual tradition, while China's centuries-old confinement of foreign trade to a single port (Canton, from 1757) produced a narrower, more competitive art market that Chinese painters could only observe from outside.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A comparative framework showing that the structure of a colonial trade relationship (open penetration vs. confined port access) directly determined the depth of cultural and economic exchange it produced.
insights.locator: Comparing the company’s two major theaters

insights.claim: George Chinnery, the only English painter to settle in the Canton region (having fled his creditors in India), trained Lam Qua, who then undercut Chinnery's prices, became Canton's most famous painter, and was in turn disowned by Chinnery, who denied ever having taught him.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A market-competition case study of a colonial-era apprentice underpricing and displacing his own teacher — an early instance of local talent out-competing the foreign incumbent once the skill transfer was complete.
insights.locator: Canton’s workshops were more competitive than India’s

themes.theme: What the art deliberately left out

insights.claim: Indigo, despite being prized by painters for rendering shadow and depth, was rarely used to depict its own exploitative production process — involving land annexation and coercive agricultural policy — reflecting British agents' deliberate ignorance of the human cost of the Company's economic activities.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A clear case of commissioned visual media systematically omitting the exploitative source of a commodity's own value chain — a pattern investors should recognize when evaluating any curated corporate or state-sponsored visual narrative.

insights.claim: Thomas Daniell's 144-print series 'Oriental Scenery,' which launched a vogue for Indian ornamentation in Britain, contains no hint of the opium- and indigo-driven famine that killed up to one-third of Bengal's population roughly two decades before the series was made, nor of opium production, despite Chinese opium addiction reaching an estimated 40 million people by 1890.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A striking magnitude data point (up to a third of Bengal's population killed by a Company-exacerbated famine, and up to 40 million Chinese opium addicts by 1890) quantifying the human cost the era's celebrated art omitted entirely.
insights.locator: yet contains no hint of the drought-fueled famine

insights.claim: Charles D'Oyly, appointed the East India Company's opium agent of Patna in 1820, used the proceeds of his opium business to finance both his personal art collection and the Behar School of Athens, an art school built directly on drug-trade profits.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: A direct, named example of illicit-commodity profits (opium) being laundered into cultural capital and institution-building, a template relevant to thinking about how commodity windfalls convert into soft power.
insights.locator: In 1820, the company appointed Charles D’Oyly

themes.theme: Art as a record of empire's turning points

insights.claim: A nine-foot-wide painting now in Tate Britain depicts Charles Malet handing a treaty to the Maratha Empire's chief minister in 1790, memorializing an alliance that helped defeat Tipu Sultan and enabled the British conquest of the Marathas.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: Shows how the Company commissioned art to commemorate the specific diplomatic-military alliances that secured its territorial conquests in India.
insights.locator: The nine-foot-wide painting, now in the Tate Britain

insights.claim: A somber circa-1795-1810 durbar painting of Nawab Mubarak al-Daula of Murshidabad, made by Indian artists, includes two boys believed to be Tipu Sultan's sons, years before the Company took them hostage after his 1792 defeat — a detail the exhibition reads as either a haunting omen or a reminder of what came after.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: An example of Indian artists using composition to encode subtle political commentary within a commissioned, ostensibly ceremonial work.
insights.locator: They are likely Tipu Sultan’s sons

insights.claim: American medical missionary Peter Parker commissioned Lam Qua to paint over 100 portraits of tumor-afflicted Chinese patients beginning in 1835 for fundraising purposes; the portraits proved medically precise enough that Yale New Haven Hospital dermatology students still use them in diagnostic exercises today.
insights.speaker: Carey K. Mott
insights.so_what: An unusual example of colonial-era commissioned art retaining direct practical utility (medical training) nearly two centuries later, illustrating the long tail of value some of this artistic output still carries.
insights.locator: Lam Qua’s portraits are apparently accurate enough
